items.title: Your CRM is not the problem
items.pov: CRM migrations keep failing because the definitions were never agreed.
items.body:
A founder told me last month she has changed CRM three times in five years and trusts none of them.

Every migration was sold internally as the fix. It never was. Sales, marketing and delivery had never agreed what a qualified deal actually is, so every system faithfully reported a different version of the truth.

I've rebuilt commercial reporting on top of HubSpot enough times to say this plainly: the tool takes the blame for a definitions problem. Agree the lifecycle first, in writing, with the people who own each stage. Then the cheapest CRM you can find will outperform the last three.

The useful question is: if your CRM vanished tomorrow, could your team write down what a qualified deal is on one page and all sign it?
items.fictionNote: FICTIONAL demonstration data. Not a real person, company or deal.

items.title: Who approved that discount?
items.pov: Discount authority is a leadership instrument.
items.body:
Sat in a deal review recently where a 22% discount had closed the quarter's biggest deal. I asked who approved it. Silence.

That silence is expensive. When nobody owns discount authority, every seller negotiates against themselves and calls it customer focus. I've introduced tiered discount authorities in a commercial team before and the effect was immediate: margins held, and oddly, deals closed faster. Buyers respect a seller who can't just keep sliding.

Still forming a view on where the tiers should sit for smaller founder-led teams. If you run one, how do you handle it today?

items.title: Sponsors renew on evidence, not logos
items.body:
Sponsorship renewals aren't a sales problem. They're an evidence problem.

When the package was logo placement and a booth, the renewal call is a negotiation about price. When the sponsor walked away with a number they could put in their own board deck, the renewal call is a scheduling exercise.

I've sold and renewed event sponsorships for years and the pattern holds everywhere: build the package around what the sponsor gets to claim afterwards, and measure it for them, because they won't.

The uncomfortable bit is that most event P&Ls can't tell you what any sponsor actually got. That's the fix, and it's cheaper than a new sales deck.
items.fictionNote: FICTIONAL demonstration data. Not a real person, company or deal.

items.title: Map the workflow before you buy the tool
items.pov: AI value comes from workflow design, not tool purchase.
items.body:
Every stalled AI story I hear from SME founders has the same shape. Tool bought. Prompt shared in Slack. Nothing changed.

The teams getting real value did something boring first: they mapped the workflow, named the steps, gave each step an owner, and then asked where a model removes drag. AI slotted into a mapped workflow compounds. AI sprinkled onto an unmapped one evaporates.

I'm doing this exercise inside my own commercial work at the moment and it's humbling how much of the drag was never the tooling.

If you've tried an AI rollout that quietly died, I'd genuinely like to hear what it looked like from the inside.

items.title: Pipeline reviews should end in decisions
items.pov: A review without stage moves is a weather report.
items.body:
I watched a pipeline review where the leader read the dashboard aloud to the room for forty minutes. Everyone nodded. Nothing moved.

A pipeline review has one job: decisions. Stage moves, kills, next actions with names on them. If the meeting ends and the pipeline looks identical, you held a weather report.

My rule now: every review ends with three lists. What moved, what died, what has a named action this week. Takes ten minutes longer to run and saves the quarter.

How does yours end?

items.title: The quiet tell in prediction markets
items.pov: Compliance-first hiring separates the serious venues.
items.body:
A pattern worth watching in prediction markets: the serious venues are hiring market integrity and compliance people before they hire growth people.

That ordering is the tell. It says who is building infrastructure for a decade and who is optimising a moment. I've watched the same split play out in other regulated categories, and the compliance-first firms looked slow right up until they were the only ones left standing.

I'm still learning my way into parts of this category, but hiring order is one of the more honest indicators I've found. Worth checking before you partner with, supply to, or bet your career on a venue.

items.title: Nobody at the founder dinner asked about models
items.pov: SMEs buy relief from pain, not AI.
items.body:
Founder dinner in Singapore last month. AI came up constantly. Not once did anyone ask which model anyone uses.

Every question was a version of "what stopped being painful". Invoicing follow-ups. Proposal drafts. CRM hygiene. The vendors pitching AI-first are answering a question nobody at that table was asking.

If you sell to SMEs, the pitch that lands is the workflow that stopped hurting, with the model as a footnote.

items.title: Events are sitting on a media business
items.pov: The audience asset earns two days a year; the media layer is the recurring revenue.
items.body:
Most event businesses monetise their audience two days a year and call the other 363 marketing.

I've built the other model: the newsletter, podcast and research layer that turns an event audience into recurring media revenue. It's not glamorous, and it outearns the event within a couple of years if you staff it like a business rather than a promo channel.

If you run events and your media line is a rounding error, that's not a market problem.

items.title: Renewals are decided in onboarding
items.pov: The first thirty days set the renewal.
items.body:
Looked back at a year of renewals once and the pattern was embarrassing in its clarity: the renewal was decided in the first thirty days. Named outcome, agreed cadence, first proof delivered. Everything after was commentary.

If your renewal conversations feel like negotiations, the fix is upstream.

items.title: The founder bottleneck is visible in the CRM first
items.pov: Operating drag is measurable before revenue feels it.
items.body:
In founder-led businesses the tell is deals sitting in a "waiting on founder" stage.

Operating drag shows up in the CRM months before it shows up in revenue. Count the deals waiting on one person's calendar and you have a leading indicator most boards never see.

[Needs one concrete anonymised example before approval.]

items.title: CFTC and Michigan: the fight moved into live trades
items.objective: Reframe the Kalshi jurisdiction story around contract finality as an operational risk.
items.body:
The prediction market argument has moved from the courtroom to the order book.

For months the Kalshi question sat where these things usually sit. Briefs, jurisdictional lines, whether an event contract is a financial product or something a state gets to license. This week it moved into live trades. The CFTC, under chairman Mike Selig, directed Kalshi to keep settling open Michigan positions while the state, through attorney general Dana Nessel, pushes the other way. That leaves the exchange holding two instructions that do not reconcile.

The interesting part sits underneath the jurisdiction fight. Contract finality has become the live question. A market only works if everyone trusts that an open position settles on its stated terms. Once settlement appears to depend on which authority is being answered that day, the product risk becomes operational rather than legal. That is the version institutional participants actually care about.

For anyone building or backing in this category, the useful read is that regulatory clarity is turning into an infrastructure requirement rather than a compliance footnote. The venues that can answer "what happens to my open position if the rules move" will have an edge that has nothing to do with liquidity.

Worth watching whether the federal and state question gets resolved as principle, or, as usual, one contract at a time.

Which authority should own the finality of a live event contract?
items.firstComment: Tag on posting: Kalshi, the CFTC, chairman Mike Selig and Michigan attorney general Dana Nessel. Primary source: [CFTC order staying the Kalshi emergency rule].

items.title: CFTC and Michigan (X): finality is the product
items.objective: Sharp live-signal version of the finality read.
items.body:
The @Kalshi story just moved from the courtroom to the order book.

The @CFTC has told Kalshi to keep settling open Michigan trades while the state, led by AG @dananessel, pushes the other way. Underneath the jurisdiction fight is a sharper question for anyone holding a position: does it still settle on the stated terms when two regulators disagree?

Contract finality is quietly becoming the product.

items.title: Forum: who carries the risk when authorities disagree?
items.objective: Practitioner prompt on operational handling of conflicting instructions.
items.body:
The CFTC has directed Kalshi to keep fulfilling open Michigan trades while the state takes the opposite view, so contract finality now reads as an operational question rather than a purely legal one.

For those of you running or building venues: what should happen to an open event contract when a federal and a state authority give incompatible instructions, and who should carry that risk? Interested in how people have actually modelled it, beyond where the law should land.
items.firstComment: Tag Kalshi, and invite the enforcement and compliance practitioners into the thread (Robert DeNault, Eric Haynes, Michael Ross) once you have confirmed handles.

items.title: Five questions Congress should ask about sports prediction markets
items.objective: Pre-hearing framing that positions Stuart on market-integrity architecture.
items.body:
On 21 July a House Agriculture subcommittee takes up sports prediction markets, and the framing of that session will matter more than the outcome.

The category has scaled faster than the rules meant to govern it. Congress now has to decide whether a sports event contract is a financial product, a gambling product, or a new thing that needs its own architecture. That decision sets who regulates, who licenses, and who carries the integrity burden.

Five questions would make the session useful rather than performative.

One, how do you identify restricted participants across teams, leagues, media, data providers and the families around them? The insider population for a sports contract is wider than most people assume.

Two, what actually separates manipulation from informed research? That line decides what surveillance is even looking for.

Three, who should hold authority over these contracts: federal derivatives regulators, states, tribes, or a shared model?

Four, what surveillance and data-sharing standards should be mandatory rather than optional?

Five, how should platforms handle contract design, settlement and consumer disclosure, given that the settlement source is where most disputes begin?

The commercial read is straightforward. The venues and vendors that already have credible answers here are the ones institutions and regulators will take seriously. The rest spend the next year reacting.

Which of the five is Congress least prepared to ask well?
items.firstComment: Tag the venues in scope, Kalshi and Polymarket, and add the confirmed witnesses once the House Agriculture Committee publishes the list. Primary source: [subcommittee hearing notice].

items.title: House hearing (X): two questions that decide the session
items.objective: Distil the hearing to its two load-bearing questions.
items.body:
A House Agriculture subcommittee takes up sports prediction markets on 21 July.

Two questions decide whether the session is useful. Can it separate market manipulation from informed research? And can it say who counts as a restricted participant across teams, leagues, media and data providers?

Almost everything else in this debate, for @Kalshi, @Polymarket and everyone building around them, is downstream of those two.

items.title: Forum: federal to tax, but not to license?
items.objective: Forum prompt on the tax-without-licensing split ahead of the hearing.
items.body:
Quick one for the practitioners here, ahead of the 21 July House hearing.

Several states are moving to tax federally regulated prediction markets while declining to license them. If a contract is federal enough to tax, what is the argument for treating it as something else for oversight? And if you run a venue, how would that split actually work day to day?
items.firstComment: Posts to the group, so no individual tag is needed. If you want a named voice, invite one of the regulatory-facing operators by name.
